headline: HOGS FALL 7.2%, CATTLE HOLDS GAINS
subheadline: Livestock complex splits as processing disruption spreads through protein markets.
lead: Lean hogs fell sharply 7.2% to $94.30 Friday, the biggest single-session drop in three weeks, as processing constraints rippled through protein markets. Live cattle held $250.07, up 3.5% for the session, creating the widest cattle-hog spread since March. The split tells you everything: cattle's got the buyer, hogs are fighting the calendar.
the_takeaway: Hog fall changes nothing about cattle fundamentals, splits the protein complex.
teaser: Hogs had their worst day in weeks while cattle held Friday's bounce.

one_number.context: The biggest single-session hog drop in three weeks came with no specific news catalyst, pointing to fund liquidation as processing constraints create calendar pressure. Live cattle's 3.5% gain the same session shows how divergent the protein complex has become since the Cargill lockout began.

sections.title: LIVESTOCK SPLITS ON PROCESSING WOES
sections.body: Live cattle closed $250.07, up 3.5% Friday, holding Thursday's technical bounce as **ready supplies stayed tight**. Feeder cattle added a token 0.1% to $353.90, acting more like they're waiting than buying. The **cattle-hog spread blew out** to $155.77, the widest since March, as processing constraints hit different proteins differently. Lean hogs fell sharply 7.2% to $94.30 with no specific catalyst, pointing to fund liquidation as calendar pressure builds. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began Tuesday May 19 continues removing 2% of weekly slaughter capacity, creating processing-constrained dynamics rather than supply-constrained.
sections.catalyst: USDA confirms second New World screwworm case in Texas cattle
sections.bottom_line: Cattle fundamentals unchanged, hogs fighting calendar math, protein complex diverging.

sections.title: GRAINS FINISH FOUR-DAY SLIDE
sections.body: Corn closed $4.17, down 1.1% to cap a **four-day slide that's cost 18 cents**. December corn at $4.46 shows the carry's still working but the front-month pressure is real. Soybeans lost 0.7% to $11.21 as export demand stays weak and **crop conditions remain favorable** despite weather concerns. Chicago wheat held $5.80, down just 0.2%, acting like the relative strength play in a weak complex. The selling has no clean catalyst, which makes it fund liquidation until proven otherwise.
sections.catalyst: Ethanol exports up 20% per USDA Secretary Rollins testimony
sections.bottom_line: Fund liquidation continues with no weather premium being priced.
sections.vs_yesterday: Grains: four-day slide extended exactly as called, no catalyst found.

sections.title: METALS FALL ON DOLLAR STRENGTH
sections.body: Gold tumbled 2.9% to $4,337 as the **dollar index pushed to $100.07**, up 0.7% and sitting at 89% of its 52-week range. Silver had it worse, dropping 5.4% to $68.94, showing how leveraged trades unwind when the dollar gets bid. The S&P 500 lost 2.6% to $7,383, confirming this was risk-off across asset classes, not just metals-specific selling. **Dollar strength is pricing out inflation hedges** as rate path expectations shift.
sections.bottom_line: Dollar strength driving metals liquidation, inflation hedges under pressure.

outside_the_pit.title: New World Screwworm Spreads to Second Texas Calf
outside_the_pit.body: USDA confirmed a second case in Zavala County, less than six miles from the first detection. The pest hasn't been seen in the U.S. for decades, and while producers shouldn't selling pressure, it's another variable in an already disrupted livestock processing chain.

outside_the_pit.title: 80% of USDA Food Assistance Workers Won't Relocate
outside_the_pit.body: The National Treasury Employees Union says more than 80% of Food and Nutrition Service staff claim they won't move if required. Administrative disruption in USDA's largest program could ripple through farm program delivery and commodity support mechanisms.

outside_the_pit.title: House Passes 2027 Ag Appropriations Bill
outside_the_pit.body: FSIS gets a $10.8 million increase to fund state inspection programs and frontline inspectors. More processing capacity oversight comes as the Cargill lockout shows how thin the margin is between normal operations and supply chain disruption.

the_more_you_know.title: When livestock spreads blow out, somebody's math is wrong
the_more_you_know.body: Friday's $155.77 cattle-hog spread is the widest since March, driven by hogs falling sharply 7.2% while cattle held a 3.5% bounce. Historically, spreads this wide don't last more than two weeks before mean reversion kicks in. Either cattle fundamentals aren't as strong as the bounce suggests, or hog selling is overdone. The processing constraints from the Cargill lockout affect both proteins, but cattle's got tighter ready supplies while hogs face more calendar pressure. Watch which side blinks first.

daily_quote.text: The ability to simplify means to eliminate the unnecessary so that the necessary may speak.

source_summary: CME Group, USDA, private weather services
